sentences.text: And the American, [POINTING] with the Allies dropping bombs on us.
Yeah, we were hidden.
There was special hiding rooms.
Oh, my name was Maria.
Maria.
No.
Oh, I never knew.
We were waiting for that day, but we never knew.
And one evening-- because the social workers had come to visit us.
And a couple times they took us [POINTING] into the town of Grenoble where we stayed in the other convent.
And we had nice meals.
And you know, in those-- when we were in Grenoble visiting the other convent, it was a day of freedom.
Somehow we could come and go.
When they would come and visit us in the convent, it was in the [FRENCH], the parlor, and there was always a nun or two attending there.
So we were careful of what was being said, And we knew we were being watched.
So we didn't have the freedom to be ourselves.
And there was [POINTING] a [INAUDIBLE] between us--
Yeah, we-- almost like a jail type of thing.
You spoke-- you couldn't hug or anything.
So--
No, that's-- no.
That's the way the orphanage operated.
And, and the doors were always locked.
You know, when we walked in, it was a huge door that opened and close.
And you know, you were, quote, 'in a safe place.' But at the same time, there was a feeling of imprisonment.
As a matter of fact, I often have dreams-- I just had a dream yesterday, and It's a recurring dream, that I'm in a place.
It's either a hospital, a morgue, or an orphanage, or a place-- and the minute I try to get out, there is a guard.
There's a door that slams.
There's a key.
There's somebody that prevents me from going-- from getting out.
I always have that recurring dream.
Well, what were we talking?
That you had these free days, where the social workers would take you into Grenoble.
And how you knew the end was coming.
We didn't know.
We didn't know.
We just didn't know.
I think we just-- there's that feeling of expectancy and that-- the nuns would keep us in the knowledge of what was going on.
They would tell us, I hear that the terrorists have arrived in such a town and in such a country.
And so one day, and I'll always remember, we were told that the two social workers had come to get us.
And to-- this time it was not a visit.
They were packing.
And we're going to go somewhere else.
And the two other girls, Lilian, Elizabeth, and myself.
So we packed.
And the social workers were, were picking us up and taking us somewhere.
And they took us to the convent of Notre Dame de Surieu in Grenoble.
And that evening we said to them, the nuns said the terrorists will be here any day, any time, and maybe tomorrow.
And they laughed and said, well, I've never heard such wishful thinking before.
Those nuns means well, but they're completely off their rock.
And it's not possible, and it's not feasible.
That the Allies could be--
That the Allies will-- and the French Partisan will be liberating Grenoble.
It's impossible.
And so we went to bed that night in that convent.
And we heard shooting.
And when we woke up in the morning, the Allies were parading.
We went in the streets, and they were parading.
And so the nuns were right.
They knew all along.
And it was a wonderful-- Then I remember we went there.
I was disappointed, I just missed de Gaulle's speech.
I-- I had hoped to hear him speak.
And--
Well, well, you know, the word de Gaulle kept being heard in the convent.
Everybody talked about him, and that he was going to be there.
And I think we read it in the paper.
I don't know.
Well, I-- you know, I can't-- I don't remember the specific.
But it was a big thing, de Gaulle being the liberator.
And so knowing that, I look forward to hear him speak.
And by the time we got to the public place, where the defile, the parade, was going through, he had finished his speech, and the crowd was dismantling.
And then we saw the Allies, the Americans, and the Allies, and the French Resistance parading in the streets.
And, and the war, for us, the war was over.
And people were dancing in the streets.
And Americans were throwing chewing gum, and candy, and chocolate, and soap all over the place.

And I think, if I remember correctly, there-- one of the punishment for women who have collaborated or who had had love affairs with Nazis or German soldiers, they-- their head was shaven and a swastika was painted on their head.
It seemed as if they took all their hatred and made them their scapegoat.
I remember those women being dragged through the streets and spit upon.
And I remember kind of an uneasy feeling about it all.
And I remember that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A feeling, mixed emotions, [SOBBING] that the war was finally over.
This was the day we all had waited for and a feeling of uncertainty.
I was 12, 13 then.
I didn't know what would come next.
I didn't know if my parents would ever return.
I think we all knew our life could never be, quote, 'normal' again, as if nothing had happened.
So there was a little apprehension about the future.
But basically, we were very joyful.
And I must say that-- I must say that the, the social workers and the nuns were very caring and supportive.
And I-- one story-- Deniz wanted me to tell that story, so I'm going to tell it.
She wanted me to tell it to the nuns of Notre Dame de Sureiu.
And so, I remember it very vividly.
We were sitting at the table having a nice lunch and looking out the window.
And I noticed that on the window there were some pieces of bread.
And the nuns had saved it for the birds.
And when they weren't looking, I stole the pieces of bread.
And I ate them.
And then the nuns and the social worker saying, I swear there was bread on the window.
I wonder what happened.
I didn't see any birds coming and getting the bread.
And I just looked somewhere else and pretended.
But I confessed to the nuns 50 years later that-- [LAUGHTER] --I was the one that stole the bread from the birds.
And I always have dreams about bread.
And I just also want to mention-- [LAUGHS] [POINTING] I was put in with peasants after the liberation.
We were separated, the three of us.
And the woman used to make very good soup, you know, nice, creamy soup.
And by that time, we were liberated, so there was plenty food.
And so when the soup was left in the pot, she would say to me, give, give the left over to the cat.
And when she wasn't looking, I'd be-- [LAUGHS]
--I'd be scraping the pot.
[LAUGHS] And that poor cat would be crying and carrying on.
And she says, did you feed the cat?
And I lied.
I said, yes.
But I didn't.
I had stolen the cats food.
[LAUGHS]
OK.
The three of us were separated.
I never saw the little girl again.
I just want to mention that Lillian and I came from working-class parents, but the little one came from upper aristocratic French Jewry.
She had beautiful, hand-embroidered underwear and very refined manners.
She talked about 'my Gouvernante,' my governess.
It was heart breaking.
I never saw her again.
I found out when I went back to France two years ago that Collette's family adopted her.
I saw Lillian on and off, and she became a bitch.
She, she was always nasty.
And she would borrow clothes from girls in the orphanage, not return them.
And she was known to just sleep around.
Each time she felt-- she was getting even for all of the hardship she endured.
And there was a hard quality about her.
I ran into her on and off.
And I, I used to hurt a lot.
She became so hard and so nasty.
And there was never any bond between us.
So after the war, I was put in a few peasant's home in the region of Grenoble.
And then we-- I was put in, in orphanages, in Jewish orphanages, in the region of Grenoble.

And my life changed drastically because being in the convent, and being a little girl, and being infatuated with Catholic rituals, liturgies, storytelling, and singing in the choir and all that, I thought maybe I'd become a nun.
I had considered conversion.
Although I must say the nuns never forced us to convert or anything.
I even said I'd like to be baptized.
And she says, wait till you're an adult and decide for yourself.
So they did treat us-- they respected. us.
So I had considered converting to Catholicism, because it seemed like an easy way out.
You know, all you have to be is be a Catholic and life is smooth from then on.
You know, no more problems.
Right.
So we were put in a Jewish orphanage with very militant, Zionist, religious directors.
And we were fed Judaism till it came out our ears and nose nonstop.
In a way, it was fun.
We had Hebrew all over-- all over the dining room and the living room.
And we were told about-- this is the first time I found out about my Jewish heritage, about Sukkot and Hanukkah and Purim.
And we celebrated all the holidays.
And we were taught Hebrew, which I had forgotten.
And we-- the couple in charge, they were a young couple.
I think she was 16.
He was 17.
And they always talked of Israel and the Promised Land.
And eventually, they left.
And they were fun.
They were wonderful.
But the directors that replaced them later on, and the monitor, or the help, they were, they-- some of them were awful.
Some of them had acquired the habit of slapping kids right and left each time-- each time the kid had a temper tantrum or, or it wasn't to their liking.
I-- when that couple left, it-- the orphanage, it, it became a drag.
Oh, and-- oh.
And also, the other orphanages in the region of Grenoble, the enthusiasm, the caring, there was no longer there.
Months.
What happened, I just felt like a languishing stalemate.
And then I wrote a letter to one of my father's friend.
I remembered the name, [SOBBING] and I remembered the address because we offered visited on a Sunday.
And I remember Place de le Republique in Paris.
That's where all--
Place de la Republique and Montparnasse, a lot of our friends live there.
So I wrote a letter not knowing if there would be an answer or not.
And I got a reply.
Not only-- I didn't get-- I got a reply.
He came to visit me.
Yes, Monsieur Erstricht.
He was a father-- my father's friend.
And he had been in a camp for foreigners.
He was not in a death camp.
So he had been a naturalized citizens of Palestine.
He was in Palestine with my parents.
And he was imprisoned as a British subject, so his life was spared.
And the Red Cross would come and gives him packages of food.
So he, he was a liberated.
And he came to visit me immediately when he got my letter.
He bought me a beautiful white hat, I remember.
And I asked him, I said I would like to go back to Paris.
And so he made arrangements with the authorities.
And I was put in a Jewish orphanage in Maison Lafitte, maybe 40, 50 miles outside of Paris, a place called Chartres.
There were two or three orphanages.
We were with the younger children.
And that's what is a nice place.
That was kinda of nice.
So I stayed there till I came to America.
I went to school.
I did well in school.
And I had very dear friends there.
And we also celebrated all the holidays, Hanukkah, and, and Purim, and Sukkot, and the storytelling, and all that.
And I had dear friends.
And I went to school.
And I did well.
And on a day off, I would visit my friends in Paris.
Well...
No.
No.
I- [SOBBING] This is one of the saddest parts of my life because the some of the children in the orphanage would get a phone call or a letter or their parents would appear on the doorstep.
And I was sure it was going to happen to me.
I was sure that hopefully both Mom and Dad, or one of them would come back and appear.
And I was always waiting.
And they never showed up.
I also went back to where we lived, Place de la Republique in Clichy.
And I got in touch with all my neighbors and left an address where I could be reached.
And I was sure they were going to come back, and I would be reunited with them.
And they never did.
Yes.
Well, I got a phone call.
In those days, having a phone was a big deal.
So they called the local post office who sent a messenger that at such a time, somebody would call for me to go-- and for the administration to be at the post office at such a time.
There would be a special phone call, that there were two gentlemen from America who we're looking for me.
And so these two gentlemen called me up.
And they happened to be what was called the Liderman brothers.
They came from a family of 20 children, 13 boys and seven girls, to my recollection.
And some of them had been in Poland and all over Europe.
And some of them had died in the death camp, and some had survived.
So those two brothers were millionaires.
They, they were not related to me.
They-- my, my aunt, who was my mother's sister, was married to one of the Liderman.
She was married to Moishe Liderman.
And he was the Fiddler on the Roof.
He played the fiddle.
And so his two millionaire brothers went to Europe after the war looking for surviving relatives.
And they found some.
Some were half families, some had perished.
And they brought their families to Los Angeles.
They provided them with an apartment, a rental, and jobs.
And she-- my aunt asked them to look me up, and they did.
So I'll always remember how I was invited.
They stayed in Hotel George V.
It was a plush place.
They ordered the steak dinner.
I had never seen steak.
Even after the liberation, we didn't see steak.
And I remember they took two bites and threw it in the trash can, the whole steak and-- the whole meal.
They had very little appetite.
And I wanted to-- you know, I knew I was taught manners.
But if nobody had looked, I would have picked it out of the trash can and put it in a bag for my friends or gobbled it up when nobody was looking.
I think the first time I became aware-- I couldn't understand how people could throw food away--
--like this.
So they told me they would make papers for me and and bring me to America.
And so in 1947, I came to America.
And I must say that, again, the theme of being a suitcase ready to be picked up, I was in school.
And I received the word that I must return.
Somebody was waiting for me.
I would be shipped on a boat, that same night or the next day, I'd be shipped on the boat to America.
So again, everything that happened in my life was-- --over night, no warning.
That's it.
Pick up your suitcase, ready to be picked up.
Exactly.
I was apprehensive about coming to America because one of the girls had gone to America and wrote sad letters.
She-- it wasn't the Promised Land.
The streets were not paved with gold.
And apparently, her relatives were very cold and unfeeling. .
And so I was a little apprehensive.
I also had been in touch with my uncle from Israel, whose photo you will see.
And he was trying to bring me over.
But at the time there was the war.
And you couldn't go in as a refugee.
So I could've changed-- if things had been different, I may have been shipped off to what became Israel.
But I think the thing now that I look back, and it had to do with a great deal of anxiety on my part, nobody asked me or ask us what we wanted to do.
I was 13, 14.
I was 15 when I came to this country.
Nobody asked me if I really wanted to go to Israel, or I wanted to go to America, or I wanted to stay in France.
It was always being decided for me.
And that was kind of scary.
And I found out years later when I met some of the girls from the orphanage-- they were women then-- [BACKROUND NOISE] --they had-- it could be my son.
They had-- [POINTS] two of them were supposed to be shipped to Israel by their uncle, and they fell in on their knees with tears saying, saying, we don't want to go to Israel.
We want to stay here.
Keep us.
And the uncle had a change of heart, and they stayed in France.
So I wasn't the only one who felt that way.
30 days on the-- on the-- what do you call those boats?
Cargo.
We landed in New Orleans.
And there were six Polish girls, orphaned.
And they were on their way to Cuba, hoping eventually to come to America.
I was put-- somebody was waiting at the boat.
And I was put on a train from New Orleans to LA.
And then I-- one of the brothers, Liderman brothers, greeted me.
And then somethings sad happened.
And that is they said to me, the two millionaires, they said, you're not part of our family.
We've done our job.
We've bought you over.
Don't ever call on us or ask for any help.
We're not going to give it to, to you.
You're not part of our family.
So I got to see them on formal occasions, maybe a wedding, or a funeral, or a-- or birthday.
But I was never to call on them.
So what did you do?
You were 15 years old and you were in Los Angeles.
I was living with my aunt and uncle.
And then became-- and that-- when I came here at the age of 15, my sufferings began all over again, because they were very abusive.
And very cruel.
Yes.
They were very cruel and abusive.
They also-- I found out later-- you know, things go on behind the scene.
They had hoped, because they were poor-- they sent me letters.
I thought they were rich, because I saw these two well-- millionaires throwing their steak away in the fanciest hotel.
I was under the illusion that my aunt and family were wealthy, and they were poor.
My uncle was unemployed.
He was a gambler and no good bum, I hate to say.
But-- and so they had hoped that the wealthy brothers would give money for my upkeep, and they didn't.
And so they took it out on me.
I had to do house work.
I was beaten.
I was starved.
I was constantly criticized.
And I, I suffered a great deal.
I ran away from them.
I was 16, And I ran away.
And I, I went to live with another family.
Anyways, there's a lot of stories to my life.
And I did what I've always done in my life when I'm feeling sad.
[CRYING] Sad, [SOBBING] and unwanted, and persecuted.
And that is, I've always had refuge in books.
And I've always done well in school, in literature particularly.
So I would go to the library, and I would borrow books, French books, famous French books, and Russian.
Books.
I was fond of Russian literature, Dostoevsky, Tolstoy.
And I did a lot of reading.
And I would listen to the radio, to the classical music station.
And that's how I felt.
I've always had good friends too.
I must say.
And here and there, I've always met good, kind adults who took interest in me.
And one of the Liderman brothers' brother, Joe Yankele, he had survived death camp.
He had lost his wife and two sons.
And he had remarried.
And so I was in touch with him.
And one day when I was visiting, I said, I'm not going back.
And so they placed me in a family where I did house work.
And the people were also very, very cold and unfeeling and stingy.
Wealthy, but stingy.
And I went to high school.
So I attended LA High.
And then later on, I did go back to my aunt.
I was forced to go back there.
Everything's a long story.
And I graduated from John Marshall High School.
And I always knew I wanted to be a French teacher.
And when I took Spanish, the Spanish teacher encouraged me in my Spanish, and encouraged me to be a Spanish teacher.
So I became a teacher of French and Spanish later on.
And because of my being mistreated, I married early.
I, I married the day after I graduated from high school.
I was 18.
And I married for the wrong reason.
He was tall, dark, and handsome.
And his goodnight kiss was fun.
I thought that happy, happy married life was in satisfying, romantic good night kiss.
And to my surprise, I discovered there's more to life than a goodnight kiss.
A goodnight kiss can turn sour, and it did.
We were very mismatched.
But what came out of this union, I have this wonderful-- my wonderful son Michael.
And I'm very thankful for Michael.
Yes.
I graduated from Berkeley with a major in French and a minor in Spanish.
And I kind of flowered.
Now, I-- I didn't-- I must say that whatever happened to me in the war has always been with me.
And I've always been open with my son about it.
I wanted him to know his grandparents.
And when he was nine on my sabbatical, I took him to Yad Vashem.
And we paid tribute there.
And I've always been open.
And he's been very supportive and caring of me.
And--
I remarried 26 years ago.
And--
I retired.
I taught school at Westchester High for nearly 30 years.
And now-- I'm now retired.
I'm studying music and creative writing.
And I just love to sing.
I love to write.
I love to travel.
I've had a very rich life in traveling.
Oh, we want to mention Deniz and, and the organization.
